text: We have now before us, I. A new author, or penman rather, or pen (if you will) made use of by the Holy Ghost for making known the mind of God to us, writing as moved by the finger of God (so the Spirit of God is called), and that is Solomon; through his hand came this book of Scripture and the two that follow it, Ecclesiastes and Canticles, a sermon and a song. Some think he wrote Canticles when he was very young, Proverbs in the midst of his days, and Ecclesiastes when he was old. In the title of his song he only writes himself Solomon, perhaps because he wrote it before his accession to the throne, being filled with the Holy Ghost when he was young. In the title of his Proverbs he writes himself the son of David, king of Israel, for then he ruled over all Israel. In the title of his Ecclesiastes he writes himself the son of David, king of Jerusalem, because then perhaps his influence had grown less upon the distant tribes, and he confined himself very much in Jerusalem. Concerning this author we may observe, 1. That he was a king, and a king's son. The penmen of scripture, hitherto, were most of them men of the first rank in the world, as Moses and Joshua, Samuel and David, and now Solomon; but, after him, the inspired writers were generally poor prophets, men of no figure in the world, because that dispensation was approaching in the which God would choose the weak and foolish things of the world to confound the wise and mighty and the poor should be employed to evangelize. Solomon was a very rich king, and his dominions were very large, a king of the first magnitude, and yet he addicted himself to the study of divine things, and was a prophet and a prophet's son. It is no disparagement to the greatest princes and potentates in the world to instruct those about them in religion and the laws of it. 2. That he was one whom God endued with extraordinary measures of wisdom and knowledge, in answer to his prayers at his accession to the throne. His prayer was exemplary: Give me a wise and an understanding heart; the answer to it was encouraging: he had what he desired and all other things were added to him. Now here we find what good use he made of the wisdom God gave him; he not only governed himself and his kingdom with it, but he gave rules of wisdom to others also, and transmitted them to posterity. Thus must we trade with the talents with which we are entrusted, according as they are. 3. That he was one who had his faults, and in his latter end turned aside from those good ways of God which in this book he had directed others in. We have the story of it 1 Kings xi., and a sad story it is, that the penman of such a book as this should apostatize as he did. Tell it not in Gath. But let those who are most eminently useful take warning by this not to be proud or secure; and let us all learn not to think the worse of good instructions though we have them from those who do not themselves altogether live up to them. II. A new way of writing, in which divine wisdom is taught us by Proverbs, or short sentences, which contain their whole design within themselves and are not connected with one another. We have had divine laws, histories, and songs, and now divine proverbs; such various methods has Infinite Wisdom used for our instruction, that, no stone being left unturned to do us good, we may be inexcusable if we perish in our folly. Teaching by proverbs was, 1. An ancient way of teaching. It was the most ancient way among the Greeks; each of the seven wise men of Greece had some one saying that he valued himself upon, and that made him famous. These sentences were inscribed on pillars, and had in great veneration as that which was said to come down from heaven. A cœlo descendit, Gnothi seauton—Know thyself is a precept which came down from heaven. 2. It was a plain and easy way of teaching, which cost neither the teachers nor the learners much pains, nor put their understandings nor their memories to the stretch. Long periods, and arguments far-fetched, must be laboured both by him that frames them and by him that would understand them, while a proverb, which carries both its sense and its evidence in a little compass, is quickly apprehended and subscribed to, and is easily retained. Both David's devotions and Solomon's instructions are sententious, which may recommend that way of expression to those who minister about holy things, both in praying and preaching. 3. It was a very profitable way of teaching, and served admirably well to answer the end. The word Mashal, here used for a proverb, comes from a word that signifies to rule or have dominion, because of the commanding power and influence which wise and weighty sayings have upon the children of men; he that teaches by them dominatur in concionibus—rules his auditory. It is easy to observe how the world is governed by proverbs. As saith the proverb of the ancients (1 Sam. xxiv. 13), or (as we commonly express it) As the old saying is, goes very far with most men in forming their notions and fixing their resolves. Much of the wisdom of the ancients has been handed down to posterity by proverbs; and some think we may judge of the temper and character of a nation by the complexion of its vulgar proverbs. Proverbs in conversation are like axioms in philosophy, maxims in law, and postulata in the mathematics, which nobody disputes, but every one endeavours to expound so as to have them on his side. Yet there are many corrupt proverbs, which tend to debauch men's minds and harden them in sin. The devil has his proverbs, and the world and the flesh have their proverbs, which reflect reproach on God and religion (as Ezek. xii. 22; xviii. 2), to guard us against the corrupt influences of which God has his proverbs, which are all wise and good, and tend to make us so. These proverbs of Solomon were not merely a collection of the wise sayings that had been formerly delivered, as some have imagined, but were the dictates of the Spirit of God in Solomon. The very first of them (ch. i. 7) agrees with what God said to man in the beginning (Job xxviii. 28, Behold, the fear of the Lord, that is wisdom); so that though Solomon was great, and his name may serve as much as any man's to recommend his writings, yet, behold, a greater than Solomon is here. It is God, by Solomon, that here speaks to us: I say, to us; for these proverbs were written for our learning, and, when Solomon speaks to his son, the exhortation is said to speak to us as unto children, Heb. xii. 5. And, as we have no book so useful to us in our devotions as David's psalms, so have we none so serviceable to us, for the right ordering of our conversations, as Solomon's proverbs, which as David says of the commandments, are exceedingly broad, containing, in a little compass, a complete body of divine ethics, politics, and economics, exposing every vice, recommending every virtue, and suggesting rules for the government of ourselves in every relation and condition, and every turn of the conversation. The learned bishop Hall has drawn up a system of moral philosophy out of Solomon's Proverbs and Ecclesiastes. The first nine chapters of this book are reckoned as a preface, by way of exhortation to the study and practice of wisdom's rules, and caution against those things that would hinder therein. We have then the first volume of Solomon's proverbs (ch. x.-xxiv.); after that a second volume (ch. xxv.-xxix.); and then Agur's prophecy (ch. xxx.), and Lemuel's (ch. xxxi.). The scope of all is one and the same, to direct us so to order our conversation aright as that in the end we may see the salvation of the Lord. The best comment on these rules is to be ruled by them.

We have here an introduction to this book, which some think was prefixed by the collector and publisher, as Ezra; but it is rather supposed to have been penned by Solomon himself, who, in the beginning of his book, proposes his end in writing it, that he might keep to his business, and closely pursue that end. We are here told, Who wrote these wise sayings, v. 1. They are the proverbs of Solomon. 1. His name signifies peaceable, and the character both of his spirit and of his reign answered to it; both were peaceable. David, whose life was full of troubles, wrote a book of devotion; for is any afflicted? let him pray. Solomon, who lived quietly, wrote a book of instruction; for when the churches had rest they were edified. In times of peace we should learn ourselves, and teach others, that which in troublous times both they and we must practise. 2. He was the son of David; it was his honour to stand related to that good man, and he reckoned it so with good reason, for he fared the better for it, 1 Kings xi. 12. He had been blessed with a good education, and many a good prayer had been put up for him (Ps. lxxii. 1), the effect of both which appeared in his wisdom and usefulness. The generation of the upright are sometimes thus blessed, that they are made blessings, eminent blessings, in their day. Christ is often called the Son of David, and Solomon was a type of him in this, as in other things, that he opened his mouth in parables or proverbs. 3. He was king of Israel—a king, and yet it was no disparagement to him to be an instructor of the ignorant, and a teacher of babes—king of Israel, that people among whom God was known and his name was great; among them he learned wisdom, and to them he communicated it. All the earth sought to Solomon to hear his wisdom, which excelled all men's (1 Kings iv. 30; x. 24); it was an honour to Israel that their king was such a dictator, such an oracle. Solomon was famous for apophthegms; every word he said had weight in it, and something that was surprising and edifying. His servants who attended him, and heard his wisdom, had, among them, collected 3000 proverbs of his which they wrote in their day-books; but these were of his own writing, and do not amount to nearly a thousand. In these he was divinely inspired. Some think that out of those other proverbs of his, which were not so inspired, the apocryphal books of Ecclesiasticus and the Wisdom of Solomon were compiled, in which are many excellent sayings, and of great use; but, take altogether, they are far short of this book. The Roman emperors had each of them his symbol or motto, as many now have with their coat of arms. But Solomon had many weighty sayings, not as theirs, borrowed from others, but all the product of that extraordinary wisdom which God had endued him with.

For what end they were written (v. 2-4), not to gain a reputation to the author, or strengthen his interest among his subjects, but for the use and benefit of all that in every age and place will govern themselves by these dictates and study them closely. This book will help us, 1. To form right notions of things, and to possess our minds with clear and distinct ideas of them, that we may know wisdom and instruction, that wisdom which is got by instruction, by divine revelation, may know both how to speak and act wisely ourselves and to give instruction to others. 2. To distinguish between truth and falsehood, good and evil—to perceive the words of understanding, to apprehend them, to judge of them, to guard against mistakes, and to accommodate what we are taught to ourselves and our own use, that we may discern things that differ and not be imposed upon, and may approve things that are excellent and not lose the benefit of them, as the apostle prays, Phil. i. 10. 3. To order our conversation aright in every things, v. 3. This book will give, that we may receive, the instruction of wisdom, that knowledge which will guide our practice in justice, judgment, and equity (v. 3), which will dispose us to render to all their due, to God the things that are God's, in all the exercises of religion, and to all men what is due to them, according to the obligations which by relation, office, contract, or upon any other account, we lie under to them. Note, Those are truly wise, and none but those, who are universally conscientious; and the design of the scripture is to teach us that wisdom, justice in the duties of the first table, judgment in those of the second table, and equity (that is sincerity) in both; so some distinguish them. For whose use they were written, v. 4. They are of use to all, but are designed especially, 1. For the simple, to give subtlety to them. The instructions here given are plain and easy, and level to the meanest capacity, the wayfaring men, though fools, shall not err therein; and those are likely to receive benefit by them who are sensible of their own ignorance and their need to be taught, and are therefore desirous to receive instruction; and those who receive these instructions in their light and power, though they be simple, will hereby be made subtle, graciously crafty to know the sin they should avoid and the duty they should do, and to escape the tempter's wiles. He that is harmless as the dove by observing Solomon's rules may become wise as the serpent; and he that has been sinfully foolish when he begins to govern himself by the word of God becomes graciously wise. 2. For young people, to give them knowledge and discretion. Youth is the learning age, catches at instructions, receives impressions, and retains what is then received; it is therefore of great consequence that the mind be then seasoned well, nor can it receive a better tincture than from Solomon's proverbs. Youth is rash, and heady, and inconsiderate; man is born like the wild ass's colt, and therefore needs to be broken by the restraints and managed by the rules we find here. And, if young people will but take heed to their ways according to Solomon's proverbs, they will soon gain the knowledge and discretion of the ancients. Solomon had an eye to posterity in writing this book, hoping by it to season the minds of the rising generation with the generous principles of wisdom and virtue.

What good use may be made of them v. 5. and 6. Those who are young and simple may by them be made wise, and are not excluded from Solomon's school, as they were from Plato's. But is it only for such? No; here is not only milk for babes, but strong meat for strong men. This book will not only make the foolish and bad wise and good, but the wise and good wiser and better; and though the simple and the young man may perhaps slight those instructions, and not be the better for them, yet the wise man will hear. Wisdom will be justified by her own children, though not by the children sitting in the market-place. Note, Even wise men must hear, and not think themselves too wise to learn. A wise man is sensible of his own defects (Plurima ignoro, sed ignorantiam meam non ignoro—I am ignorant of many things, but not of my own ignorance), and therefore is still pressing forward, that he may increase in learning, may know more and know it better, more clearly and distinctly, and may know better how to make use of it. As long as we live we should strive to increase in all useful learning. It was a saying of one of the greatest of the rabbin, Qui non auget scientiam, amittit de ea—If our stock of knowledge by not increasing, it is wasting; and those that would increase in learning must study the scriptures; these perfect the man of God. A wise man, by increasing in learning, is not only profitable to himself, but to others also, 1. As a counsellor. A man of understanding in these precepts of wisdom, by comparing them with one another and with his own observations, shall by degrees attain unto wise counsels; he stands fair for preferment, and will be consulted as an oracle, and entrusted with the management of public affairs; he shall come to sit at the helm, so the word signifies. Note, Industry is the way to honour; and those whom God has blessed with wisdom must study to do good with it, according as their sphere is. It is more dignity indeed to be counsellor to the prince, but it is more charity to be counsellor to the poor, as Job was with his wisdom. Job xxix. 15, I was eyes to the blind. 2. As an interpreter (v. 6)—to understand a proverb. Solomon was himself famous for expounding riddles and resolving hard questions, which was of old the celebrated entertainment of the eastern princes, witness the solutions he gave to the enquiries with which the queen of Sheba thought to puzzle him. Now here he undertakes to furnish his readers with that talent, as far as would be serviceable to the best purposes. They shall understand a proverb, even the interpretation, without which the proverb is a nut uncracked; when they hear a wise saying, though it be figurative, they shall take the sense of it, and know how to make use of it.The words of the wise are sometimes dark sayings. In St. Paul's epistles there is that which is hard to be understood; but to those who, being well-versed in the scriptures know how to compare spiritual things with spiritual, they will be easy and safe; so that if you ask them, Have you understood all these things? they may answer, Yea, Lord. Note, It is a credit to religion when men of honesty are men of sense; all good people therefore should aim to be intelligent, and run to and fro, take pains in the use of means, that their knowledge may be increased.

The fear of the Lord is laid down as the foundation of all true knowledge. Reverence for God, submission to His will, and a deep awareness of His holiness form the beginning of wisdom. Those who reject instruction and despise correction show themselves to be fools. The teachings of parents are to be esteemed as valuable ornaments, bringing both honor and protection to those who receive them.

Young people are warned against the enticements of sinners who promise quick gain through violence and deceit. Though such paths may appear profitable, they ultimately lead to destruction. Those who lie in wait for others end up ensnaring themselves. The pursuit of unjust gain blinds the heart and destroys the soul, and therefore such company must be firmly resisted.

Wisdom is portrayed as calling aloud in the streets, urging all to turn from their folly. She offers correction and promises understanding to those who listen. Yet many refuse, choosing instead their own stubborn ways. Because they reject wisdom, calamity comes upon them suddenly. However, those who heed her voice will dwell securely and be free from fear, showing the blessing of obedience and the danger of neglecting God’s instruction.

This passage urges the diligent pursuit of wisdom. It is not gained casually but through earnest seeking, as one searches for hidden treasure. When the heart is inclined toward understanding and cries out for discernment, God grants knowledge. The result is a deeper understanding of the fear of the Lord and the knowledge of God.

All true wisdom comes from the Lord. He gives knowledge and understanding to those who seek Him. He preserves the upright and guards the paths of justice. Those who receive His wisdom are enabled to discern righteousness and integrity, and their hearts are protected from evil by understanding.

Wisdom delivers from evil men who speak perverse things and delight in wrongdoing. It also protects from the immoral woman, whose flattering words lead to destruction. Those who follow such paths forsake righteousness and bring ruin upon themselves. Wisdom provides discernment to avoid these dangers.

The chapter concludes by contrasting the destiny of the righteous and the wicked. The upright will dwell in the land and remain secure, while the wicked will be cut off. This reinforces the importance of walking in the path of righteousness and avoiding the ways of evil.

Trust in the Lord with all the heart is here set forth as the essence of true wisdom. Believers are called to rely not on their own understanding but to acknowledge God in all their ways. The promise is that He will direct their paths. Obedience to God’s commandments brings peace, favor, and stability.

Humility and reverence for God are urged, along with turning away from evil. Honoring the Lord with one’s substance brings blessing, and accepting His discipline is evidence of His love. Just as a father corrects his son, so the Lord disciplines those He delights in.

Wisdom is described as more valuable than silver or gold. Those who find it possess true riches, including long life, peace, and honor. Wisdom is rooted in God’s creative work, showing that to walk in wisdom is to walk in harmony with the order established by God.

The chapter closes with practical exhortations: maintain sound wisdom, avoid unnecessary fear, and act with integrity toward others. Kindness, justice, and humility are commended. The Lord blesses the righteous but opposes the wicked, reinforcing the moral order established by God.

A father’s instruction is emphasized, urging children to seek wisdom above all else. Wisdom is portrayed as a precious possession that brings honor and exaltation. Those who embrace it are guided and protected throughout life.

The path of the righteous is contrasted with the way of the wicked. The righteous grow in light and clarity, while the wicked stumble in darkness. The exhortation is to remain firmly on the path of righteousness and avoid the ways of evil.

Guarding the heart is presented as essential, for it determines the course of life. Words, thoughts, and actions must be carefully directed. By focusing on righteousness and avoiding distractions, one remains on the path that leads to life.

A strong warning is given against sexual immorality. The seductive words of the immoral woman may seem pleasant, but they lead to bitterness and destruction. Those who fall into such sin bring shame and loss upon themselves.

Faithfulness in marriage is commended as both honorable and joyful. A man is to delight in his own wife and remain devoted to her. The Lord sees all ways of man, and those who pursue sin are ensnared by it. The chapter concludes with a reminder that lack of discipline leads to ruin.

These verses warn against becoming entangled in financial obligations for others without wisdom. Acting as surety for a neighbor may seem kind, but it can lead to bondage and distress. The wise course is to humble oneself and seek release quickly, showing diligence and urgency to avoid ruin.

The sluggard is directed to observe the ant, which labors diligently without supervision. This teaches the value of foresight, discipline, and hard work. Laziness leads to poverty and sudden want, like an armed man overtaking the unprepared.

Here is a description of a wicked person whose speech and actions are deceitful. Such individuals sow discord and delight in evil. The Lord hates these things, especially a proud look, a lying tongue, and hands that shed innocent blood. These sins disrupt both personal righteousness and community peace.

The importance of parental instruction is emphasized again, especially in guarding against sexual immorality. The adulteress is portrayed as dangerous and destructive. Unlike theft, which may be repaid, adultery brings lasting shame and consequences. The jealous rage of a wronged spouse makes reconciliation impossible.

The call is to treasure God’s commandments and keep them close, as one would guard something precious. Wisdom is to be treated as a close relative, guiding and protecting from temptation, especially from immoral influences.

A vivid illustration is given of a young man lacking judgment who is led astray by a seductive woman. Her words are flattering and persuasive, but her intentions are destructive. The young man follows her impulsively, not realizing the danger, like an ox going to the slaughter.

The chapter concludes with a warning to listen and avoid the path of temptation. Many strong men have fallen because of such sin. Her house is described as the way to hell, leading down to the chambers of death.

Wisdom is portrayed as calling openly and publicly, offering instruction to all who will listen. Unlike hidden temptation, wisdom is clear and accessible. Her teachings are more valuable than silver and gold, and those who seek her will find true riches.

Wisdom dwells with prudence and provides knowledge and discretion. She hates pride and evil and is associated with righteousness and justice. Kings and rulers govern rightly through wisdom, and those who love her are rewarded with enduring riches.

Wisdom is described as present at creation, rejoicing in God’s works. This passage highlights the eternal nature of wisdom and its role in the ordering of the world. It reflects the harmony and joy found in God’s design.
A final appeal is made to heed wisdom’s instruction. Those who find wisdom find life and favor from the Lord, while those who reject her harm themselves. To hate wisdom is to love death.

Wisdom prepares a feast and invites the simple to come and learn. This symbolizes the provision of truth and understanding for those willing to receive it. Those who accept the invitation will gain life and walk in understanding.

A contrast is made between the response of the wise and the foolish to correction. The wise accept rebuke and grow in knowledge, while the foolish reject it and respond with hostility. The fear of the Lord remains the foundation of wisdom.

Folly is personified as a loud and seductive woman who invites the naive to her house. Though her offer seems appealing, it leads to death. Those who follow her do not realize that her guests are in the depths of the grave.

The proverbs of Solomon begin, contrasting the wise and foolish son. A wise child brings joy, while a foolish one causes grief. Diligence leads to prosperity, while laziness results in poverty. The importance of timely labor is emphasized.

Blessings come upon the righteous, while the wicked conceal violence in their speech. The wise store up knowledge, but the foolish speak recklessly. Words have power to preserve or destroy, and wisdom governs the tongue.

Wealth can provide security, but poverty brings vulnerability. However, righteousness is more valuable than riches. The words of the righteous nourish many, while the foolish lack understanding and perish.

The blessing of the Lord brings true wealth without sorrow. The righteous find joy in doing good, while the wicked delight in evil. The fear of the Lord prolongs life, but wickedness shortens it. The chapter closes by contrasting the speech and destiny of the righteous and the wicked.

Honesty in dealings is greatly valued by the Lord, while deceit is detestable. Integrity guides the upright, but the crookedness of the wicked destroys them. Riches cannot save in the day of wrath, but righteousness delivers from death. The righteous are preserved, while the hopes of the wicked perish.

The righteous are delivered from trouble, often while the wicked fall into it themselves. Hypocrisy destroys relationships, but knowledge preserves the just. Communities rejoice when the righteous prosper, but mourn when the wicked rise. Wise counsel brings safety, while rash commitments bring harm.

Gracious and generous people gain honor and blessing. The merciful do good to their own souls, while the cruel harm themselves. True reward comes from righteousness, not deceitful gain. Those who pursue good find favor, but those who seek evil will encounter it.

Generosity leads to increase, while withholding leads to poverty. Those who refresh others will themselves be refreshed. Trusting in riches leads to downfall, but the righteous flourish. Ultimately, righteousness is rewarded, and wickedness is judged.

A love for instruction is a sign of wisdom, while hatred of correction reveals foolishness. The upright gain favor from the Lord, but the wicked are condemned. Stability comes through righteousness, while wickedness leads to instability and ruin.

Contentment with modest means is better than pride with lack. The righteous care even for their animals, while the wicked are cruel. Diligence leads to provision, while idleness leads to poverty. Wisdom restrains anger, but fools quickly reveal their shame.

Truthful speech promotes justice, while falsehood brings deception. Words can wound or heal, and the tongue of the wise brings health. The Lord delights in truthfulness. The path of righteousness leads to life, and there is no death in it.

A wise son receives instruction, but a scoffer rejects rebuke. Guarding one's speech preserves life, while careless words bring destruction. Righteousness protects the upright, but sin overthrows the wicked.

Appearances can be deceiving—some pretend to be rich yet have nothing, while others appear poor yet possess great wealth. Discipline and patience are emphasized, and hope deferred makes the heart sick, but fulfilled desire brings life.

Despising instruction leads to destruction, while fearing the commandment brings reward. The teaching of the wise is a fountain of life. Walking with the wise leads to wisdom, but companionship with fools leads to harm.

Trouble pursues sinners, but blessings reward the righteous. A good man leaves an inheritance to his children’s children. Discipline is an expression of love, and the righteous are satisfied while the wicked lack.
A wise woman builds her house, while a foolish one destroys it. Upright living reflects reverence for God. The wise seek knowledge and understanding, while fools lack discernment.

Wisdom enables one to understand his way, but foolishness leads to deception. The inner life of a person is known to God. There is a way that seems right but leads to death. The righteous are rewarded, while the wicked face consequences.

The poor are often despised, but kindness toward them honors God. Righteousness exalts a nation, while sin brings reproach. Wisdom and patience are marks of strength, and the fear of the Lord provides confidence and refuge.
A gentle answer turns away wrath, while harsh words stir up anger. The tongue of the wise brings healing, but the speech of fools causes harm. God sees all things, both good and evil.

The Lord values righteousness over religious formality. The prayers of the upright delight Him. A peaceful life with little is better than great wealth with trouble. The fear of the Lord brings life and contentment.

Patience calms conflict, while anger stirs it up. The path of the sluggard is difficult, but the upright walk with ease. Wisdom leads to joy and proper speech. Humility and the fear of the Lord lead to honor.

Man proposes, but God disposes. Though people make plans, the final outcome belongs to the Lord. Therefore, commit your works unto Him, and He will establish your thoughts and direct your paths according to His sovereign will.

The Lord has made all things for His purposes, even the wicked for the day of judgment. Pride is detestable to Him and will not go unpunished. Those who trust in Him will find mercy. A man’s heart plans his way, but the Lord directs his steps.

Kings must rule with justice, for their authority is given by God. Righteous lips are pleasing to rulers, and integrity strengthens a kingdom. Favor from a king is like life itself, bringing blessing and security.

Wisdom is far more valuable than gold. The wise avoid evil and guard their souls. Pride leads to destruction, but humility brings honor. Pleasant words are like honey, sweet to the soul and healing to the body.

There is a way that seems right to a man, but its end is death. Hard work is driven by need, but wickedness brings ruin. The Lord governs even the casting of lots, showing His control over all events.

Better is a dry morsel with peace than a house full of feasting with strife. God tests hearts, and those who mock the poor insult their Maker. Justice and righteousness are valued above outward prosperity.
Family is a crown of honor, and children reflect their parents. Fools delight in wrongdoing, but wisdom avoids it. It is better to face a bear robbed of her cubs than a fool in his folly.

Returning evil for good brings lasting trouble. Quarreling must be stopped early before it grows. A joyful heart promotes healing, but a broken spirit dries the bones. The perverse heart finds no good.
A foolish child brings grief to parents. Silence and restraint are marks of wisdom. Even a fool is considered wise when he keeps quiet, showing that wisdom is often found in self-control.
Isolation often leads to selfishness. Fools delight in expressing their own opinions rather than seeking understanding. Words can wound deeply, spreading like fire and bringing destruction.
Laziness leads to ruin. The name of the Lord is a strong tower where the righteous find safety. Wealth may seem like security, but true protection is found in God alone.
A case seems right until another side is heard. Words have power over life and death. A man who finds a wife finds a good thing, and true friendship is found in one who sticks closer than a brother.
Integrity is better than wealth gained through dishonesty. Desire without knowledge leads to error. Wealth attracts many friends, but the poor are often neglected.
Wisdom preserves life. False witnesses will not go unpunished. Laziness brings poverty, while diligence leads to satisfaction and stability.
Keeping God’s commands leads to life. Kindness to the poor is lending to the Lord, and He will repay. The fear of the Lord leads to life, bringing rest and protection.
Laziness leads to waste and hunger. Discipline is necessary for wisdom. Judgments are prepared for scoffers, and punishment awaits fools.
Wine and strong drink can lead to mockery and error. A righteous man walks in integrity, and his children are blessed after him. The king’s authority is powerful and must be respected.
A king who judges righteously removes evil. Honest weights and measures are required by God. Wisdom is more valuable than gold and precious stones.
Avoid becoming entangled in another’s debt. Deception may seem sweet at first but leads to trouble. Do not repay evil with evil, but trust the Lord to bring justice.
Dishonest scales are detestable to the Lord. A person’s steps are directed by Him. Discipline, though painful, brings correction and ultimately leads to righteousness.

The Lord directs the hearts of rulers as streams of water. Righteousness and justice are more pleasing to God than sacrifice. Pride leads to downfall, but diligence leads to prosperity. The wicked pursue evil and show no mercy, while the wise seek understanding. God's judgment is certain, and no human wisdom can stand against Him.

A good name is more valuable than riches. Train up a child in the way he should go. The rich and poor meet together under God’s sovereignty. Discipline and humility lead to honor. Wisdom teaches prudence in life, relationships, and work, warning against laziness and poor associations.

Wisdom warns against greed, drunkenness, and immoral living. True riches are not found in wealth but in righteousness. Parents are encouraged to guide their children in truth. A disciplined life leads to peace, while indulgence leads to sorrow and destruction.

Do not envy the wicked, for their success is temporary. Wisdom builds strong lives and households. The righteous may fall but rise again, while the wicked fall into ruin. Laziness leads to poverty, but diligence leads to provision. Justice and truth must guide all actions.

These proverbs highlight wisdom in leadership, humility, and self-control. A gentle answer turns away wrath. Patience and restraint bring honor. Pride leads to humiliation, while humility brings elevation. Self-control is essential, for a person without it is like a city without walls.

Folly is out of place for a fool just as honor is undeserved. The chapter warns against engaging in the arguments of fools and highlights the danger of laziness and deceit. A lying tongue and a deceitful heart bring destruction, while wisdom calls for restraint and discernment in dealing with others.

Do not boast about tomorrow, for life is uncertain. Faithful friends are a blessing, offering honest correction and support. Diligence in work and care for responsibilities lead to stability. The chapter emphasizes the value of relationships, humility, and wise stewardship.

The righteous live with boldness, while the wicked flee in fear. Obedience to God's law brings blessing, while rebellion leads to ruin. Integrity and honesty are honored, while greed and corruption destroy. Those who trust in the Lord will prosper spiritually.

Repeated rejection of correction leads to destruction. Righteous leadership brings stability, while wicked rule brings chaos. Discipline is necessary for wisdom, and fear of man is a trap, but trust in the Lord brings security. Justice and righteousness must guide all areas of life.

The sayings of Agur reflect humility before God and the limits of human understanding. The chapter includes numerical sayings that highlight patterns in creation and human behavior. It calls for contentment, self-control, and reverence for God’s wisdom.

King Lemuel’s teachings emphasize righteous leadership and justice. The chapter concludes with the description of the virtuous woman, highlighting her wisdom, diligence, kindness, and fear of the Lord. True beauty and worth are found in godly character.
